The wood is not dry. It breathes.  

It has a grain that slopes like a hill behind a window, catching the light when the sun slips low, turning the edges into warm gray. There’s a faint hum beneath the floorboards — not noise, but presence — a vibration that pulses through the heel of your foot when you stand too still.

log.seeker_text:
The smell of wet earth after rain.  
Not the damp, heavy kind that clings to walls, but the sharp, green release — like the world just breathed out. It’s not just soil. It’s the first sigh of roots pushing through cracked pavement, the way the air tastes like forgotten things waking up. I stand barefoot on a patch of grass that hasn’t seen sunlight in weeks / The shape of a single drop of rain on a hot sidewalk at 3:17 p.m. — perfect, still, round, like a black pearl suspended in the heat. It doesn’t fall. It just *holds*. The air above it bends slightly, like glass. I watch it for ten seconds.
